By employing N-doped lignin-derived carbon quantum dots as a carrier for Pt single atoms, in conjunction with CdS, a synergistic enhancement of photocatalytic hydrogen production was achieved [180]. Under visible light excitation, electrons generated from CdS accumulated on Pt single atoms due to the interaction between Pt-N bonds and S atoms. Pt single atoms effectively reduced the overpotential for H2 evolution and serve as active sites for the dissociation of H2O molecules, reducing H+ to H2 (Fig. 20 a). The hydrogen evolution rate in this ternary photocatalytic system, was 68.8 times higher than that of pristine CdS NRs, and it remained stable even after continuous irradiation for 60 h (Fig. 20b). In addition, the photocatalytic hydrogen production activity of carbon quantum dots prepared from lignin as a carbon source was significantly higher than that of other carbon sources (NCCDs from citric acid, NCCDs from glucose, and NChCDs from chitosan) (Fig. 20c). This superiority can be attributed to lignin's rich aromatic conjugated structure and the highest carbon content, enabling the formation of highly conductive carbon quantum dots that maximized synergistic effect with Pt single atoms.

Besides HER, oxygen evolution reaction (OER) is the other crucial half-reaction of water splitting. Hayat et al. [216] designed a novel photocatalyst with outstanding performance in both HER and OER through molecular engineering and calcination. In their design, lignin was grown on CN polymerized with 2,6-diphenyl imidazole (CNP-L). With the addition of organic monomers and lignin, the electron mobility within the CN π-conjugated framework was increased. The incorporation of monomers and lignin may introduce new surface states that can capture charges to reduce the recombination of electron-hole pairs by transferring charges from the excited CN to integrated monomers and lignin (Fig. 20 d). In the presence of the electron sacrificial agent AgNO3, the oxygen production rates of CNP5.0 and CNP-L0.5 reached 9 (45.68 μmol/h) and 11(55.91 μmol/h) times that of CN (4.9 μmol/h) (Fig. 20 e, f).

Most research on photocatalytic water splitting focuses on the half-reaction of HER or OER in the presence of co-catalysts and sacrificial agents. Indeed, achieving overall water splitting, where both hydrogen and oxygen are simultaneously produced from water under sunlight, is a significant goal in photocatalysis. To realize this, it's essential to modify and regulate the band structure and electron structure of photocatalysts using lignin. This approach can enhance the efficiency of charge separation and utilization, ultimately improving the overall water splitting performance.

Lignocellulosic biomass, as the most abundant renewable carbon source on the earth, holds enormous potential for the production of high-value chemicals through photocatalysis [217–221]. Due to the mild reaction conditions of photocatalysis, coupled with the unique reaction modes induced by light-generated reactants, it is possible to selectively cleave target chemical bonds or functionalize specific functional groups while preserving others [222]. Lignin, being dominant aromatic resource in nature, holds promise for the production of high-value fuels and aromatic compounds like phenols, aromatic aldehydes, and aromatic acids, through depolymerization. To realize high-value utilization of lignin, photocatalysis plays a crucial role in improving the economic and technical feasibility of lignin depolymerization. The design of a photocatalytic lignin depolymerization process, characterized by optimal selectivity and durability, is both pressing and challenging. The ultimate goal is to establish a scalable photo-biorefinery process that effectively transforms lignin into valuable products.

Over the past serval years, it's interesting to see that researchers have been already delving into the utilization of lignin-based photocatalytic materials for lignin depolymerization. For example, in 2018, Srisasiwimon et al. [147] prepared lignin-carbon-modified TiO2 and conducted photocatalytic conversion of lignin under UV light. Their study demonstrated the potential of lignin-derived carbon-modified TiO2 for lignin depolymerization, achieving a lignin conversion rate of 40.28 % within 5 h. GC-MS analysis identified several high-value chemicals such as vanillin, acetyl vanillin, 4-hydroxybenzaldehyde, and benzoic acid among the photocatalytic products. However, the efficiency and product yields were relatively low, indicating scope for improvement in future research endeavors. Unfortunately, the mechanism behind this conversion had not been proposed due to the complexity of lignin. In 2021, Liu et al. [176] prepared P-doped lignin-derived carbon quantum dots and loaded them on g-C3N4. Under simulated solar radiation, the chemical bonds of lignin model compounds were broken under the attack of various ROS, yielding valuable lignin-derived platform chemicals, such as formyl phenyl acetate, benzaldehyde, and benzoic acid. By focusing on lignin model compounds, this study simplified the intricate process of lignin depolymerization and provided insights into the value-added utilization of lignin through photocatalysis.

Starting with representative lignin model compounds provides a valuable opportunity to clarify the mechanism of photocatalytic lignin transformation, thus giving insights into the conversion of native lignin. Understanding the selective cleavage of C-O bonds that are abundant in lignin, is pivotal for the efficient depolymerization of lignin into aromatics. Wu et al. [223] conducted a thorough investigation into the selective photocatalytic oxidative cleavage mechanism of lignin C-O bonds using SLS-modified Fe3O4/TiO2. By analyzing the resulting products and EPR spectra, they were able to elucidate the transformation mechanism of 2-Phenoxy-1-phenylethanone (PP-one) and p-benzyloxyphenol (PBP).

Initially, 2-phenoxy-1-phenylethanol (PP-ol) was oxidized to PP-one, and then the Cβ-O bond of PP-one underwent dehydrogenation by the hole, forming a benzyl ketone radical and -OPh specie. Subsequently, in the presence of H+, the benzyl ketone radical was oxidized by superoxide anion to generate benzoic acid and -OPh specie, resulting in the formation of phenol (Fig. 21 a). PBP underwent dehydrogenation to form a phenoxide anion, which then formed a phenoxyl radical A by donating an electron through a hole. Next, radical A transformed into phenoxyl radical B through quinone resonance, and the activated carbon atom was likely the active site for subsequent bond cleavage. Quinone intermediates may originate from molecular rearrangement of radical B induced by UV irradiation, providing evidence for the presence of these radicals. Subsequently, benzyl alcohol and benzoquinone originated from the C-O band cleavage over the superoxide anion and H+ (Fig. 21b).

Regarding the depolymerization mechanism of lignin, identifying reaction intermediates and free radical species can be achieved either by utilizing in situ observation techniques to capture the surface adsorption behavior of the catalyst or by using atomic-scale DFT calculations to derive the details of the chemical reactions [225]. However, there is still a lack of direct thermodynamic or kinetic evidence to support the proposed reaction mechanisms. Moreover, due to the complex heterogeneous structure of natural lignin and the involvement of intricate bond cleavage and reorganization in the reactions, the photocatalytic depolymerization of lignin faces critical challenges. Currently, research on lignin-based photocatalysts for lignin depolymerization is still insufficient. Some photocatalysts with good performance, such as metal sulfides, typically exhibit slow reaction kinetics and a decline in performance over time [226]. This creates an opportunity for lignin-based photocatalysts to improve reaction efficiency and reproducibility. In addition, it is critical for the high value utilization of lignin to promptly separate the target oxidized products from the reaction system to avoid the further oxidization degradation of the products.

The overuse of fossil fuels results in excessive carbon dioxide emissions, underscoring the urgent need for efficient carbon capture and utilization technologies [227]. Photocatalytic reduction of CO2 to produce chemical fuels mimics the process of photosynthesis and holds significant importance in establishing a sustainable carbon-neutral economy. CO2 photocatalytic conversion represents a crucial pathway for mitigating the effects of greenhouse gas and addressing energy crises, with products including CO, CH4, formic acid, and methanol [228]. One key aspect of photocatalytic CO2 reduction is the adsorption of CO2 on the surface of the photocatalyst [229]. It has been demonstrated that lignin-modified photocatalysts can effectively enhance the adsorption capacity of CO2 on the photocatalyst. For instance, Gao et al. [230] enhanced the photocatalytic activity of CO2 reduction to CO by loading lignin-derived CQDs onto TiO2 nanosheets. The improved performance was attributed not only to the CO2 adsorption capacity, which was 1.4 times higher compared to pristine TiO2, but also to the improved charge separation efficiency resulted from the migration of photoelectrons from TiO2 to the surface of the lignin-based CQDs during the reaction process.

Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is an environmentally friendly oxidant widely applied in disinfection, chemical production, wastewater treatment, and bleaching processes. Besides, it serves as a relatively stable energy carrier that boasts easier storage compared to hydrogen [231]. In contrast to traditional manufacturing methods, such as the well-established anthraquinone oxidation process, which involve multi-step hydrogenation reactions, novel approaches offer alternatives that mitigate significant energy consumption and generation of polluting waste [232]. Therefore, the photocatalytic synthesis of H2O2 using water (H2O) and oxygen (O2) as raw materials and solar energy as the energy source offers considerable attention in terms of environmental sustainability, cost-effectiveness, safety, and overall green chemistry principles, holding significant promise for addressing the demand for hydrogen peroxide while minimizing environmental impact and resource consumption.

The efficiency of photocatalytic hydrogen peroxide production can be enhanced by the following strategies. The first one is to add sacrificial agent as proton donor in the reaction to accelerate the charge separation and improve the oxygen reduction efficiency. Suitable sacrificial agents certain extent characterized by relatively simple structures and long-chain polyhydroxy alcohols such as glycerol > isopropanol > ethanol > methanol [233]. The sacrificial agent needs to be separated from the hydrogen peroxide solution at the end of the reaction, which undoubtedly increases the operating cost. The second one is to modify the photocatalyst to adjust its energy band structure and enhance its charge separation performance. For this strategy, modifying the band structure of semiconductors by lignin can improve their light absorption and redox properties, making them more efficient photocatalysts for H2O2 production.

The approach proposed by Gopakumar et al. [224] utilizing sustainably sourced lignin as a substrate to grow BiOBr semiconductor nanosheets through the hydrothermal method. Since only lignin-loaded BiOBr (LBOB) can fulfill both direct and indirect pathways for H2O2 generation, LBOB exhibited a higher H2O2 production rate compared to chitosan, graphene-loaded BiOBr, and pristine BiOBr under simulated seawater conditions (0.6 M NaCl) (Fig. 21d). Molecular dynamics simulation analysis suggested that some chains of lignin molecules readily release hydrogen to nearby carriers and water molecules in the solution (part of the top layer of BiOBr). Frequent hydrogen exchange between lignin, substrate, and water molecules was evident, indicating significant reactivity at the oxide/organic interface, where sodium ions and oxygen molecules accumulate (Fig. 21e). This innovative approach not only underscored the potential of lignin-based materials in sustainable photocatalysis, but also emphasized the effectiveness of lignin as a promoter in enhancing the photocatalytic synthesis of H2O2, particularly in challenging environments such as seawater.

There is another strategy of loading solid proton donors onto photocatalysts to enhance charge separation while making it easier to collect the produced hydrogen peroxide [234]. Fortunately, it was demonstrated that lignin as a photocatalyst satisfies the thermodynamic conditions for H2O2 production [92]. The hydroxyl group on Cα can be oxidized to carbonyl group facilitating the reaction and meanwhile providing protons, despite most of the protons for H2O2 production come from the solvent water oxidized by holes (Fig. 21f and g) [92,131]. For example, lignin extracted from eucalyptus by the ethanol pulping method with higher content of β-O-4 ether bonds and better photocatalytic properties retains the ether bonds after the reaction, which is a fundamental condition for its practical recycling application [131]. However, in practical aqueous applications, contaminants will disrupt the ether-bonding protective mechanism, leading to mineralization of lignin. In addition, in salt-rich and weakly alkaline seawater, Na ions interfere with the coordination of O2 to lignin, and it is difficult to utilize protons in the presence of OH−, which affects the efficiency of H2O2 production. Nevertheless, studies on lignin photocatalytic properties still provide precious value for the green economic transition of lignin. Finally, Table 2 summarizes recent research on alternative applications of lignin-based photocatalytic materials beyond pollutant degradation.

Considering the realization of highly efficient photocatalysis and the high-value utilization of lignin, it is crucial for the development of lignin-based photocatalytic materials. This review provides a comprehensive overview of the synthesis methods, enhancement mechanisms, and applications of lignin-based photocatalytic materials. Sustainably sourced lignin, boasting rich functional groups and high carbon content, emerges as an excellent candidate template and carbon carrier for enhancing photocatalyst performance through efficient dispersion, optimized electron transfer and extended light absorption. Notably, the proven photocatalytic activity of lignin is a distinguishing feature compared to other biomass raw materials. Undoubtedly, lignin exhibits tremendous application potential in photocatalysis. However, further exploration and overcoming certain challenges are required for the practical application of lignin-based photocatalytic materials.
1)
Lignin, with high carbon content and functional groups, stands out as an ideal biomass source for the synthesis of modified photocatalytic materials. Various methods such as solid-phase, liquid-phase, sol-gel, evaporation-induced self-assembly, electrospinning, along with calcination and hydrothermal methods, have been developed to enhance the quality of lignin-based photocatalytic materials. Balancing the lower preparation cost with superior photocatalyst performance, including the dispersibility of semiconductor photocatalysts and the interfacial contact between carbon materials and semiconductors, poses a primary challenge. Moreover, when the synthesis processes developed in the laboratory are applied to practical industrial settings, issues related to equipment may arise. The costs associated with raw materials, equipment, energy consumption, and waste treatment for various synthesis methods need to be systematically evaluated. Methods with long reaction times and low yields are not suitable for practical production, and should be replaced by synthesis methods that can be integrated with continuous automated production. The environmental impact mainly involves two factors: energy consumption and the emission of toxic substances. What does not need to be considered is the green sustainability of the raw materials, which is a significant positive impact of lignin. Therefore, future development should focus on increasing product yield, reducing process costs, and minimizing environmental pollution.
2)
The primary differences in the chemical structure between different types of natural lignin lie in the content of aromatic rings, oxygen-containing functional groups, and crosslinking degree. These factors will determine the charge transfer efficiency, porosity, and stability of the synthesized photocatalytic materials. Untreated lignin often encounters some obstacles during the synthesis of lignin-based photocatalytic materials, including the lack of positively charged functional groups and poor water solubility, which are unfavorable for achieving sample uniformity [235]. Amination and sulfonation modifications effectively overcome this issue, and elements that are lacking in some unmodified lignin can also be incorporated into the photocatalytic materials, potentially resulting in unexpected effects [236–238]. Furthermore, when lignin is directly used as a photocatalyst, the content of β-O-4 bonds and methoxy groups plays a crucial role in photocatalytic H2O2 production. Therefore, gaining a deeper understanding of the specific structural features of lignin and their positive effects on specific photocatalytic reactions will provide guidance for the optimization and modification of future lignin extraction methods.
3)
Currently, most research has focused on the characterization of photocatalyst performance and testing of reaction efficiency, with the mechanisms of material synthesis remaining unclear. Efforts should be made to refine the material preparation process, with an emphasis on proposing the synthesis mechanisms to better control the process, improve catalyst quality, and avoid energy waste. Moreover, the research on the interface charge transfer dynamics between lignin-based carbon materials and semiconductors is still not well-defined. Therefore, employing high-resolution transient spectroscopy techniques for precise tracking of charge carriers or conducting simulation calculations can aid in elucidating the charge transfer pathways between semiconductors and carbon materials, thus proposing a comprehensive reaction mechanism.
4)
Although lignin can indeed directly act as a photocatalyst, the diversity of lignin sources and its complex heterogeneity pose significant challenges to the advancement of research. Controlling lignin's heterogeneity requires a deep understanding of the functions corresponding to specific lignin structures. Characterization techniques with limited resolution are often insufficient to clarify the relationship between specific lignin structures and catalytic performance. Meanwhile, achieving modification of specific lignin functions faces challenges related to selection complexity and insufficient precision. Currently, lignin fractionation is a simple and effective strategy aimed at separating lignin according to molecular weight, improving the uniformity in both chemical structure and molecular weight to reduce lignin's heterogeneity [239,240]. However, the impact of lignin fractionation on its photocatalytic performance is not well understood. Therefore, controlling lignin's heterogeneity also requires the development of various high-resolution characterization techniques and theoretical calculations to further explore the relationship between multifunctional structures and photoactivity. Establishing a comprehensive evaluation and screening system will be an important direction for future research.
5)
Nowadays, numerous reported works of lignin-based photocatalytic materials have demonstrated highly promising in photocatalytic water treatment. However, there is insufficient exploration into the underlying reaction mechanisms, which can be clarified through combination of more accurate characterization techniques and simulation calculations. Additionally, despite the considerable efficiency in degrading pollutants, the intermediates produced during degradation may possess toxic properties, potentially even more toxic than the original pollutants. Therefore, it is essential to study the mineralization degree or detect intermediates for toxicity analysis in relevant research. Moreover, conducting test in various simulated water bodies and even in real wastewater is also encouraged to comprehensively assess the effectiveness of photocatalytic materials.
6)
In fact, incorporating sacrificial agents is a common strategy for photocatalytic HER and OER. However, it becomes economically challenging if the cost of the sacrificial agent exceeds the value of the gas produced. While the addition of sacrificial agents serves for theoretical validation, it will be a milestone to achieve the complete water splitting through regulating lignin-based photocatalytic to maximally utilize photogenerated electrons and holes. Perhaps, using organic compounds as hole sacrificial agents appears to be a more economical and ideal reaction. This approach simultaneously achieves the selective oxidation of organic compounds and H2 production, which provides a two-in-one solution for biomass valorization and energy production, thereby increasing its practicality and economic viability.
7)
Nitrogen fixation and carbon dioxide reduction are currently among the hot topics in photocatalytic research. Although various semiconductor [241,242] and carbon-based materials [243–245] have been extensively studied as photocatalysts for nitrogen fixation and CO2 reduction, research on lignin-based photocatalysts in this field is still in its early stages. For traditional semiconductor materials, the potential of lignin lies in enhancing their photoelectric properties to meet the requirements for practical production efficiency. Among various carbon-based materials, lignin is an excellent carbon source, making its substitution for other carbon materials a promising approach. It is certain that the appealing properties of lignin-based photocatalytic materials are likely to capture the attention of researchers in these cutting-edge fields. This interest will probably drive further exploration of efficient and environmentally friendly photocatalytic technology, thereby advancing the development of solar-chemical energy conversion.
8)
Recyclability is a crucial factor for photocatalysts in practical applications. In real-world use, photocatalysts encounter challenges like structural damage from photo-corrosion, catalyst agglomeration, reactant adsorption, and separation difficulties. Studies have indicated that lignin-based photocatalytic materials offer better recyclability than the original photocatalysts, primarily due to their stable structure. Its inherent sustainable source characteristic means that even if the catalyst's performance slightly declines, the low cost of the material itself makes its recycling more economically feasible. Furthermore, although the impact of lignin itself when directly used as a photocatalyst has not been studied, the aforementioned advantageous properties offer great potential for its practical application in production processes. Despite photocatalytic technology holds immense promise, its current industrialization prospects remain limited. Building upon experiments, it is imperative to further explore the scaling up light sources, reactors, and controlling the experimental conditions to maintain consistency with laboratory results post-scaling, thereby enabling a smoother transition to industrialization.

Ying Xu: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Wei Li: Investigation. Wenjie Sui: Writing – review & editing. Ting Xu: Writing – review & editing. Guanhua Wang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Jinguang Hu: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Chuanling Si: Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.
